LAW AND MOTION 301, JAN-22-2025 RE: PLAINTIFF'S APPLICATION FOR A TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER. NO APPEARANCE WAS MADE BY ANY PARTY, COUNSEL, OR INTERESTED PERSON. THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFF KARENA APPLE FENG'S APPLICATION FOR A TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER AND OSC RE PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION IS GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. SEE WORD MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING. AS THE PREVAILING PARTY, FENG SHALL PREPARE A VERBATIM PROPOSED ORDER FOR THE COURT'S SIGNATURE, SHALL SERVE NOTICE OF THE SIGNED ORDER ON ALL PARTIES WHO HAVE APPEARED IN THE CASE, AND SHALL FILE THE PROOF OF SERVICE WITH THE COURT. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN. CLERK: ROBERT WOODS. NOT REPORTED. (301/CVA)

REMARK: HEARING HELD ON PLAINTIFF EX PARTE APPLICATION FOR TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER. THE MOVING PARTY APPEARED IN PERSON. THE COURT EXPLAINED TO THE PLAINTIFF THE REASONS WHY IT WOULD NOT BE HEARING THE APPLICATION: 1. THE MOVING PARTY FAILED TO DELIVER A COURTESY COPY (SF LR 2.7(B)). 2)THE MOVING PARTY DID NOT SIGN UP FOR COURTCALL. THE COURT PROVIDED INSTRUCTIONS TO THE MOVING PARTY AND INFORMED THEM TO CHECK THE SAN FRANCISCO SUPERIOR COURT WEBSITE FOR ANY EX PARTE APPLICATION REQUIREMENTS. THE COURT INSTRUCTED THE MOVING PARTY TO RE-FILE AND ENSURE COMPLIANCE WITH THE COURT RULES WHEN SUBMITTING AN EX PARTE APPLICATION. VISITING JUDGE CINDEE MAYFELD; CLERK: M. MACADANGDANG; NOT REPORTED (302/CM)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-03-2026 Continued To Jul-01-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE RE PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION
Off Calendar Apr-10-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE RE PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Karena Apple Feng's application for a preliminary injunction ordering the return of the 2014 Mercedes-Benz ML350 4MATIC (VIN: 4JGDA5HBOEA374039) that is the subject of this suit ("Car") is denied. The court dissolves the temporary restraining order previously issued on January 22, 2026.

In deciding whether to issue interim relief, the court must evaluate two interrelated factors. "The first is the likelihood that the plaintiff will prevail on the merits at trial. The second is the interim harm that the plaintiff is likely to sustain if the restraining order were denied as compared to the harm that the defendant is likely to suffer if the order were issued. [] The trial court's determination must be guided by a mix of the potential-merit and interim-harm factors; the greater the plaintiff's showing on one, the less must be shown on the other to support a restraining order. Of course, the scope of available preliminary relief is necessarily limited by the scope of the relief likely to be obtained at trial on the merits. A trial court may not grant a restraining order, regardless of the balance of interim harm, unless there is some possibility that the plaintiff would ultimately prevail on the merits of the claim." (Church of Christ in Hollywood v. Superior Court (2002) 99 Cal.App.4th 1244, 1251-1252 [citation simplified].)

Defendant Fifth Third Bank N.A. provides evidence that Feng is in default on her loan agreement on the car. (DeJong Decl., para. 4 & Ex. B.) Feng offers no contrary evidence. The court concludes that Feng has not shown a possibility of prevailing on the merits at trial of her claim that the car was improperly seized.

Proposed order submitted. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)
